# Brief: Tom (PERSONA-006)
## Upcoming Meeting: 2026-04-03

# Pre-Meeting Brief: Tom (PERSONA-006 — Tax-Conscious Resident)
**Meeting date: 2026-04-03**

---

## Since Last Meeting

No new evidence has surfaced since the April 2 board meeting. That's notable in itself.

The April 2 meeting confirmed the 6% ceiling as the superintendent's FY27 proposal — a $257/year increase on the average South Portland home — but the board still did not take a formal vote. They are now heading into the April 7 city council meeting without an adopted budget. That is an unusual posture, and it means the next 48–72 hours before that council meeting are procedurally live.

The absence of news between April 2 and April 3 means one of two things: either this is a quiet interlude before the April 7 council session, or a meeting is being called specifically to resolve the adoption question the board deferred again on April 2. Either way, Tom goes into April 3 without new information — but also without anything that changes his baseline. The 6% number is confirmed. The board has not closed the loop. And three board members (Richardson, Holman, DeAngelis) are still on record wanting to push the ask higher.

The silence since April 2 is not reassurance. It is unfinished business.

---

## Open Questions

These are the threads Tom is carrying in — phrased the way he actually thinks about them:

- **Why hasn't the board formally voted on the budget yet?** They've confirmed the 6% ceiling twice in meetings. At some point, "the superintendent recommends 6%" and "the board has voted 6%" are two different things. Which one are they actually bringing to the council on April 7?
- **If Richardson, Holman, and DeAngelis still want more than 6%, when does that show up — before or after the council meeting?** They didn't just disappear. Are they making their move at April 3, or are they waiting to see what the council says on April 7?
- **What happens procedurally if the board shows up to the council without an adopted budget?** Does the council just hear the superintendent's recommendation and move forward, or does going without a formal board vote weaken the district's position — or give the council an opening to push back harder?
- **That $300K–$750K in possible new state money — is that in or out of the 6% proposal?** If it comes through, does the district apply it to close the structural gap, or does it get used to restore cut positions? I've seen them use one-time money to defer hard decisions before. I need to know which way they're going with it.
- **Nobody has produced the full per-household number yet.** School increase plus sewer rate increases plus police and fire bond plus Mahoney — all four, stacked, on one property. Has anyone run that math and put it in front of the public? Because I'd like to know what the next three to four years actually costs me.
- **Labor contracts still grow costs faster than 6% per year.** The finance director said it himself on March 23 — 6% is mathematically impossible to sustain in steady state. So what's the FY28 plan? Another crisis cycle? Or is someone actually going to address the contract structure?
- **The school administration still hasn't delivered the breakdown of 82 added positions.** They promised it to the city council in February. It's April. Where is it?
- **If the council agrees to loan the school district money to rebuild its fund balance, what are the repayment terms?** And how does that add to my total bill?

---

## Agenda Implications

No agenda has been published for the April 3 meeting. Based on where things stand, here is what Tom should be prepared for.

**Most likely scenario: a procedural gap-fill before the April 7 council meeting.**

The board deferred formal budget adoption on April 2. April 7 is five days away. If the board needs a formal vote on the record before they walk into the council chamber, April 3 is a plausible moment for that. Tom should watch for whether a special meeting has been called — or whether the April 3 date refers to a city council work session or another municipal body.

**What this means for Tom specifically:**

If this is a school board session, the main question is whether the formal 6% vote finally happens — or whether the push for a higher number resurfaces. That vote is the only thing standing between "superintendent recommends 6%" and "the board has actually committed to 6%." Those are not the same thing.

If this is a city council session, Tom's attention should shift to whether the council treats the April 7 meeting as a rubber stamp or as a genuine review. His experience since December is that the council produces verbal concern and no binding action. The April 7 meeting is one of the few remaining moments where the council could enforce a lower ceiling — or signal they won't.

**What Tom should listen for:**

- Any board member reopening the 6% question before a vote is taken
- Any discussion of presenting the council with a range (e.g., "6% to 8%") rather than a fixed number
- Any framing of the $300K–$750K state money as justification for restoring cuts rather than reducing the levy ask
- Whether the combined-burden question — all four simultaneous cost pressures — is raised by anyone in an official capacity, or continues to be treated as a private homeowner concern

**Questions Tom should prepare:**

- *"Has the board formally voted to adopt the FY27 budget at 6%, or is the council on April 7 receiving a superintendent's recommendation without board authorization?"*
- *"If the new state funding comes through, will it be applied to reduce the levy ask to taxpayers or to restore positions that were cut?"*
- *"When will the breakdown of 82 added positions be made available — the one promised to the city council in February?"*

---

## Watch For

- **Whether a formal board vote occurs or is deferred again.** This is the single most important procedural signal. If the board goes to the city council on April 7 without a formal vote, Tom should understand this as the board preserving room to maneuver on the number — not a clerical oversight.

- **Board members Richardson, Holman, and DeAngelis.** These three have been the consistent voices pushing above 6%. Watch whether they speak at this meeting and whether their tone has shifted since the April 2 confirmation of the 6% ceiling. Any renewed pressure is a signal that the ceiling is softer than the finance director's statement implied.

- **Any mention of a "range" being presented to the council.** If the district shifts from "we are asking for 6%" to "we are presenting the council with options between 6% and X%," that is the ceiling reopening under a different framing.

- **How the $300K–$750K in potential state funding is described.** Watch for whether it is framed as deficit relief (applied to close the structural gap) or as restoration funding (used to bring back cut positions). The difference directly affects whether Tom's $257/year figure holds.

- **Whether anyone — board, staff, or public commenter — raises the combined per-household burden across all four cost pressures.** If that number surfaces officially for the first time, it matters. If it still doesn't surface, that absence is itself a data point Tom should note and be prepared to raise in public comment.

- **The finance director's language around the 6% ceiling.** On April 2, the finance director confirmed it as the superintendent's proposal. Watch whether that framing tightens (the board votes and owns it) or loosens (it remains a recommendation without board authorization).

- **Any mention of fund balance loan terms from the city to the school district.** If this surfaces, the repayment structure and timeline are critical inputs to Tom's total burden calculation — and no public body has laid that out yet.

- **Procedural tone from the chair.** If the chair moves to extend discussion rather than call a vote at an obvious decision point, that is a signal — familiar from several prior meetings — that the board is not ready to close the loop and that the April 7 council appearance will be procedurally awkward.
